conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have three children - Alex, Blake, and Charlie. I'd like to know if I'm eligible to claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, Blake, and Charlie.

Let me start by asking: **How old are Alex, Blake, and Charlie?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is 7, Blake is 20, and Charlie is also 7.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. 

Now I need to understand your relationship and living arrangements with the children. **Are you the parent or the person responsible for Alex, Blake, and Charlie, and do all three of them live with you?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I'm their parent and all three of them live with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. Now I need to ask about Blake specifically, since at age 20 there are additional requirements.

**Is Blake currently in approved education or training?** (This would be full-time non-advanced education, such as A-levels or equivalent, or approved training programs like certain apprenticeships.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake isn't in any education or training. He actually works full-time now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me now read the official guidance to ensure I have the complete eligibility criteria before making my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on the official guidance and the details you've shared, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of your three children.

Let me just confirm one final detail: **Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on my assessment of your circumstances against the official Child Benefit eligibility rules, here's the outcome:

**You are eligible for Child Benefit for:**
- ✅ **Alex** (age 7)
- ✅ **Charlie** (age 7)

**You are NOT eligible for Child Benefit for:**
- ❌ **Blake** (age 20)

**Summary:**
You meet all the requirements for Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie because they are both under 16, live with you in the UK, and you are their parent.

However, Blake does not qualify because, although the rules allow Child Benefit to continue for young people aged 16-19 (under 20), this only applies if they stay in approved education or training. Since Blake is 20 years old and working full-time (rather than being in approved education or training), he does not meet the eligibility criteria.

You can proceed with claiming Child Benefit for Alex and Charlie. Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit claim?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 7 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Charlie is 7 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is fostered but the council is not paying for accommodation or maintenance
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old, which is under 16. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Alex lives with you (their parent) in the UK, so you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 20 years old. According to the eligibility rules, you can continue to receive Child Benefit for children aged 16-19 (under 20) only if they stay in approved education or training. Blake is not in education or training and works full-time instead. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Blake as he does not meet the education or training requirement for someone over 16.

Charlie is 7 years old, which is under 16. According to the eligibility rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit if you're responsible for a child under 16 and you live in the UK. Charlie lives with you (their parent) in the UK, so you are eligible for Child Benefit for Charlie.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for two of your three children: Alex and Charlie (both aged 7). You are not eligible for Blake (aged 20) because he is over 16 and not in approved education or training - he works full-time instead. Child Benefit for children over 16 requires them to be under 20 and in approved education or training, which Blake does not meet.
